**Franco Turigliatto** (born 13 December 1946 in [Rivara](/wiki/Rivara "Rivara"), Piedmont) is an Italian politician.

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franco_Turigliatto&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Franco Turigliatto began in 1966 his political militancy in [Mario Capanna](/wiki/Mario_Capanna "Mario Capanna")'s Student Movement, with the occupation of Palazzo Campana in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"). In 1969 he joined the [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International"). With [Livio Maitan](/wiki/Livio_Maitan "Livio Maitan") he led the Lega Comunista Rivoluzionaria (Revolutionary Communist League) until 1989, when this group merged into [Proletarian Democracy](/wiki/Proletarian_Democracy "Proletarian Democracy"). In 1991 he took part in the construction of the [Communist Refoundation Party](/wiki/Communist_Refoundation_Party "Communist Refoundation Party"). Turigliatto became a member of the national leadership and leader of an internal minority of the PRC, [Red Flag](/w/index.php?title=Bandiera_Rossa_Association&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bandiera Rossa Association (page does not exist)"), led by Maitan. As a member of the National Direction of PRC he worked for several years, within the Labor Department, of the Large Factory division, closely following the crisis of [FIAT](/wiki/FIAT "FIAT") and going daily to the gates of the Mirafiori factories in Turin.

In 2006 Turigliatto was elected to the Senate and as member of PRC, it was part of the majority that supported the [Prodi II Cabinet](/wiki/Prodi_II_Cabinet "Prodi II Cabinet"). On 21 February 2007 he didn't participate together with the PdCI's Senator [Fernando Rossi](/wiki/Fernando_Rossi "Fernando Rossi") to the vote on the motion by Foreign Minister [D'Alema](/wiki/Massimo_D%27Alema "Massimo D'Alema") regarding the Government's foreign policy. On 1 March 2007 the National Guarantee College of PRC decided to expel Turigliatto from the party for 2 years.[[1]](#cite_note-1) After his expulsion from the PRC, Turigliatto joined the new party [Critical Left](/wiki/Critical_Left "Critical Left"), born from a split in the PRC trozkist area.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In the 2008 general election Turigliatto was candidate to the Senate with Critical Left, but the party gained no seats.

In 2013, after the dissolution of Critical Left, Turigliatto founded [Anticapitalist Left](/wiki/Anticapitalist_Left_(Italy) "Anticapitalist Left (Italy)"), of which he became the leader.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
